attributes.stem:
An 85-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a three-month history of an itchy red rash on his back and thighs and the development of a number of painless blisters on his feet over the past 36 hours. He has a past medical history of ischaemic stroke, severe aortic stenosis, angina pectoris, hypertension, type 2 diabetes and osteoarthritis. His medications include clopidogrel, felodipine, metformin, gliclazide, sublingual GTN spray PRN and paracetamol PRN.

On examination, he appears comfortable with vital signs in the normal range. Examination of the skin reveals a confluent erythematous maculopapular rash of the lower back and upper thighs/groin region. Several moderate-large sized bullae are noted on both feet, but particularly on the right side, with the largest bulla measuring approximately 10cm in size (Image). Some smaller bullae are seen on both arms, with no evidence of any others elsewhere on the skin. Examination of the oral mucosa and conjuctivae is normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The correct answer to this question is bullous pemphigoid. This patient presents with a three-month history of an erythematous rash, along with blisters that have formed in the last 36 hours, most prominently on the feet. This history is classic of bullous pemphigoid, which is an autoimmune disease that typically occurs in elderly patients, particularly those with a history of cerebrovascular events and other neurological diseases. Prior to the onset of blisters, there is a prodromal phase during which patients will typically report the development of an itchy eczematous/urticarial rash. This phase can last months. During the bullous phase, blisters can develop on areas of skin already affected by the pre-existing rash, or on apparently normal skin. On examination, patients are generally systemically well. Bullae are most often located on the flexural aspects of the extremities, lower trunk and abdomen. They can be several centimetres in size, as is demonstrated in this case. Mucosal involvement is uncommon, but does occur.

Pemphigus vulgaris is an intraepidermal blistering condition. They do not present with subepidermal blisters and hence they often present with erosions that are crusted without any intact blister. It is also a condition in which there is mucosal involvement, often of the oral mucosa.

Bullous diabeticorum is an uncommon complication in patients with diabetes where they develop occasional subepidermal blisters often located on distal limb. This is not associated with any symptoms or rash or any other skin change.

Toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN) is a severe skin disorder that lie on a spectrum of severity. SJS and TEN are characterised by mucosal involvement, extensive skin necrosis and epidermal detachment. SJS is classified by <10% body surface area (BSA) involvement, SJS-TEN overlap syndrome 10-30% BSA and TEN >30% BSA. Patients will present as unwell, with extensive desquamation and often haemodynamic instability from fluid losses. Blisters can occur, however they are not the hallmark feature. Mortality for these conditions is high (up to 10% for SJS and at least 30% for TEN). As this patient appears well, with no evidence of mucosal lesions, haemodynamic instability or skin desquamation, SJS and TEN are incorrect answer options.

Bullous impetigo is a blistering skin infection caused by Staphylococcus aureus. Common in younger children, lesions will often form in between folds of skin, where the environment is warm and moist (e.g. axillae, groin creases). This patient's three-month history of maculopapular rash and acute blister formation is not consistent with bullous impetigo, making this answer option incorrect.
learningPoints: Bullous pemphigoid is an autoimmune disease that typically occurs in elderly patients, particularly those with a history of cerebrovascular events and other neurological diseases.
references: Bağcı IS, Horváth ON, Ruzicka T, Sárdy M. Bullous pemphigoid. Autoimmunity reviews. 2017;16(5):445–55. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28286109/

Cole C, Gazewood J. Diagnosis and treatment of impetigo. American Family Physician. 2007;75(6):859–66. Available at: https://www.aafp.org/pubs/afp/issues/2007/0315/p859.html#:~:text=There%20is%20no%20standard%20treatment,for%20patients%20with%20extensive%20disease.
